title: Experimental Design - Fill in the Blank

questions.question: Name the type of variable the experimenter actively manipulates.
questions.blank_sentence: In an experiment, the variable you manipulate is the ___ variable.
questions.explanation: The independent variable (IV) is what the researcher manipulates, such as noise level or cognitive load. Its effect is then measured on the dependent variable.

questions.question: Name the type of variable the experimenter measures.
questions.blank_sentence: The outcome you measure, such as reaction time, is the ___ variable.
questions.explanation: The dependent variable (DV) is the measured outcome, such as reaction time, accuracy, or eye movements. It is expected to depend on the manipulated IV.

questions.question: Name the kind of relationship that experiments, unlike correlations, can establish.
questions.blank_sentence: Because they manipulate an IV, experiments can establish a ___ relationship.
questions.explanation: Manipulating the IV while controlling other factors lets experiments support causal claims. Correlational designs can only show association, not causation.

questions.question: Name the error of detecting an effect that is not actually there.
questions.blank_sentence: A false positive, rejecting a true null hypothesis, is a Type ___ error.
questions.explanation: A Type I error is a false positive controlled by alpha, conventionally set at .05. A Type II error, by contrast, is missing a real effect.

questions.question: Name the quantity equal to 1 minus beta.
questions.blank_sentence: The probability of correctly detecting a real effect, 1 - beta, is called ___.
questions.explanation: Power (1 - beta) is the chance of correctly rejecting a false null. It rises with larger samples, bigger effects, less variability, and a larger alpha.

questions.question: Name the technique that has Group 1 do A then B while Group 2 does B then A.
questions.blank_sentence: To cancel the average order effect in a within-subjects study, use ___.
questions.explanation: Counterbalancing reverses condition order across groups so the average order effect cancels out. For three or more conditions, a Latin-square design counterbalances systematically.

questions.question: Name a variable that systematically differs between conditions and could explain the results.
questions.blank_sentence: A variable that differs with the conditions and could explain the effect is a ___.
questions.explanation: A confound, such as word frequency in a reading-time study, offers a rival explanation for the results. Controls like randomisation and matched stimuli reduce confounds.

questions.question: Name the design that manipulates two or more independent variables at once.
questions.blank_sentence: Manipulating two or more IVs simultaneously defines a ___ design.
questions.explanation: A factorial design crosses multiple IVs, for example noise by task difficulty, and is analysed with factorial ANOVA. A 2x2 factorial produces four condition combinations.

questions.question: Name the statistic that conveys the magnitude of an effect beyond its p-value.
questions.blank_sentence: Cohen's d and eta-squared are measures of ___.
questions.explanation: Effect size reports how large an effect is, which a p-value alone never conveys. Two studies with the same p can have very different effect sizes.

questions.question: Name what happens when participants guess the hypothesis and adjust their behaviour.
questions.blank_sentence: Participants guessing the study's aim and changing how they respond are showing demand ___.
questions.explanation: Demand characteristics arise when participants infer the hypothesis and adjust their behaviour. Fillers, cover stories, and blinding help reduce them.
